pattern: Any new cycle a redundant edge creates is checkable in isolation, because combining several even-weight cycles through symmetric difference always stays even -- so a parity-tracking union-find can accept or reject each edge on the spot without ever re-examining earlier ones.
hints: Track each node's cumulative XOR-weight relative to its component's root, the way a weighted union-find tracks distances -- merging two different components is always safe and never creates a cycle.

The interesting case is when an edge's two endpoints are already in the same component: that edge would close exactly one new cycle, whose total weight is determined by the two endpoints' already-known parities plus the new edge's own weight.

Because every cycle in the whole graph can be built as a combination of these individual closing edges' cycles, and combining even-weight cycles always yields an even-weight cycle, checking each new edge's own closing condition in isolation is enough -- no need to re-check previously accepted edges.

constraints.note: edges[i] = [ui, vi, wi] with 0 <= ui < vi < n, wi is 0 or 1, all edges distinct

edge_inputs.description: minimum node count, no cycle possible yet with two edges
closing an odd-weight triangle -- the closing edge must be rejected
closing an even-weight triangle -- the closing edge is accepted
two separate components merged later by one edge, always accepted
a redundant edge that would create an odd cycle from an all-zero-weight component
repeating the same edge twice with a consistent weight -- second copy still accepted
repeating the same edge with a conflicting weight -- second copy rejected
a longer chain where the final closing edge keeps the total even

stress.description: a large random graph near the maximum node and edge budget
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(3, min(size, 50000))
    m = min(n * (n - 1) // 2, 50000)
    seen = set()
    edges = []
    while len(edges) < m:
        u = rng.randint(0, n - 2)
        v = rng.randint(u + 1, n - 1)
        if (u, v) in seen:
            continue
        seen.add((u, v))
        w = rng.randint(0, 1)
        edges.append([u, v, w])
    return [n, edges]
